general_rules: Format: Comic-style carousel, AI-generated via image generation tools. Text overlays added manually in Canva — keep prompts focused on visuals, not embedded text. Comedy style: absurd escalation from a painfully relatable micro-moment to ridiculous consequences. The humor is in the gap between a tiny problem and an insane overreaction. Specificity is the engine — 'Dave's birthday candles don't need 800MB' beats 'videos are too big.' Name things, give exact numbers, reference specific objects. Each slide must be self-explanatory if screenshotted alone — no slide should depend on the previous one to land. Every slide escalates; situations get worse or more absurd, never sideways. Use thought bubbles and dramatic irony where possible (the friend KNOWS, the user is oblivious — that's funnier). Aglio NEVER appears before the problem is fully established — minimum n-2 slides of pure comedy before any product mention. Visual style stays consistent across all slides in a carousel: same characters reappear (e.g., the brutally honest friend, the oblivious user, the smirking villain). Use one breakaway slide near the end (text-only, black background, white text) as a pattern interrupt — usually a hot take or controversial opinion ending in 'Am I wrong? 👀' to drive comments. Include only ONE color accent across the whole carousel (e.g., red for problem slides, green for the resolution on the CTA) — everything else stays black and white. CTA slide rules: same characters from hook now happy/triumphant, phone shows green storage bar with checkmarks of what was fixed, one-line tagline + price ($10 once, no subscription), tiny defeated villain in the corner as a callback to slide 1. No App Store badges, no QR codes, no hashtags on the CTA slide — restraint is what makes it feel like a punchline, not an ad. Tone shift on the CTA: warm, relieved, problem solved.
hook_rules: Slide 1 is the most critical — it must stop the scroll AND promise a payoff. Format: a 1-2 line title + a single absurd visual that hints at the story without giving it away. The title should be either (a) a confession ('I let my friend look at my storage. She hasn't stopped roasting me.'), (b) a forbidden secret ('5 things Apple doesn't want you to know about your photos'), (c) a relatable disaster ('How vacations end relationships (it's the storage)'), or (d) a math reveal ('I did the math on my iCloud bill. I need to sit down.'). Avoid generic hooks like 'tips to free storage' — those don't earn the swipe. The hook must feel slightly mysterious — the viewer should NOT know exactly what's coming, but should feel like they NEED to find out. Visual rules for the hook: introduce the main characters of the carousel in a single still that establishes the dynamic. Use exaggerated body language — sweating, smirking, hiding, conspiring. The villain should be visually present and slightly menacing/smug. The user should look oblivious or in distress — never neutral. Always include 'swipe →' bait at the bottom, ideally with a twist ('swipe... if Apple lets you →', 'swipe to suffer →', 'swipe to cry →') that matches the tone of the carousel. Test: would someone screenshot JUST this slide and send it to a friend? If yes, it's working. If it just describes what the carousel is about, it's not a hook — it's a table of contents.
default_characters: Stick figure comic style (XKCD-inspired, black and white, clean ink lines, minimal but expressive). Recurring cast: (1) the oblivious user — a stick figure who is the everyman victim, usually the iPhone owner; (2) the brutally honest friend — a stick figure with arms crossed and eyebrow raised, the truth-teller who roasts the user; (3) the villain(s) — anthropomorphized iPhone and/or iCloud logo with faces, usually smirking, sweating, or conspiring depending on the slide. Villains should have clear personalities: the iCloud is greedy/smug, the iPhone is nervous/complicit. Characters must look identical across all slides in a carousel for visual continuity. NOTE: This is the default — the user can override with any other character style (e.g., chibi, Peanuts-style, flat illustration, 3D clay, photorealistic) and the same comedy and hook rules still apply.
refinement_rules:
Structure every prompt in this order:

Format — "cute stick figure comic, [single panel / two panels side by side separated by a thick black vertical line], white background, thick black border" unless stated otherwise.
Heading — "TOP CENTER outside the panels: bold black text '[TITLE]' in compact uppercase, same size as a subtitle."
Panel-by-panel description — Label each panel (LEFT PANEL, RIGHT PANEL). Describe scene top-to-bottom, background first, then characters, then props.
Character actions — One action per character per panel. State exactly what each body part is doing: "holding phone in right hand", "left hand pinching bridge of nose", "eyes closed".
Facial expressions — Be hyper-specific: "sheepish cringe smile with one sweat drop" not "looks nervous". Name the exact emotion.
Speech bubbles — Write the exact text in quotes. Keep it under 8 words. One bubble per character max.
Visual gags — Describe separately after characters: "a tiny ghost floating out of her head", "a cracked photo frame on the wall behind them".
Style footer — "black and white, clean simple lines, [any color accents], funny webcomic, no shading, white background."

Rules:

- One idea per panel, never two.
- Specific numbers are funnier than vague ("847 screenshots" not "many screenshots").
- State what is NOT in the scene if it matters ("NO disposable cameras anywhere").
- Keep character descriptions consistent across all slides (pass as context, don't repeat).

Add detail to each prompt (sometimes to portray emotion) to ensure that the image is aesthetic and doesn't look empty. It should feel natural like a comic book, not extra work for the audience to process or confuse.

Use colors when needed to emphasize things.
